clinical_history: 85 year old man admitted with shortness of breath and a stable ECG without change from previous month. Cardiac enzymes were negative. The ECG shows evidence of all of the following EXCEPT?
diagnosis: This ECG shows sinus tachycardia at rate of about 100 bpm with marked left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular block (QRS duration=0 .11 second). However, complete LBBB is NOT present. AV conduction is slightly prolonged (PR interval=0.22 second). There is virtually complete loss of R wave progression across the precordium with slight ST segment elevations, as well as Q waves in 1 and aVL.  This patient had a long history of severe coronary artery disease, prior MIs and heart failure, with a markedly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 10-15%. Transthoracic echocardiogram showed severe global left ventricular wall motion disorder, with a very large apical ventricular aneurysm. The presence of pathologic Q waves and persistent ST elevations weeks to months after an MI is a relatively specific, but not sensitive sign of a left ventricular aneurysm. (A prominent R wave in lead aVR, as present here, has also been reported with this condition, probably due to left anterior fascicular block combined with loss of apico-lateral QRS forces. Another ECG sign of ventricular aneurysm, not seen here, is an rSr' pattern in left-sided leads.)
